Comparing Against estdaR and spdyn

Source: vignettes/prior-art-comparison.Rmd
prior-art-comparison.Rmd

estdaR and spdyn are prior R implementations of spatial distribution dynamics methods. They are useful as methodological comparators and oracle checks. griddy targets a different interface: long sf panels, tidy outputs, and map-ready objects.

The main difference is not that griddy computes an unrelated statistic. It is that the unit identity, time index, class labels, transition labels, and spatial lag intervals stay attached to the result.

library(griddy)
library(dplyr)
library(sf)
library(spData)
library(spdep)
library(tidyr)

data(us_states, package = "spData")
data(us_states_df, package = "spData")

states <- us_states |>
  left_join(us_states_df, by = c("NAME" = "state")) |>
  filter(!NAME %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii", "Puerto Rico")) |>
  arrange(NAME)

panel <- states |>
  select(NAME, median_income_10, median_income_15, geometry) |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("median_income_"), names_to = "year", values_to = "income") |>
  mutate(year = if_else(year == "median_income_10", 2010L, 2015L))

listw <- nb2listw(poly2nb(states, queen = TRUE), style = "W")

spatial <- spatial_markov(panel, NAME, year, income, listw = listw, k = 5)

spatial$transitions |>
  select(id, from_time, to_time, lag_class, transition, spatial_lag) |>
  head()
## # A tibble: 6 × 6
##   id          from_time to_time lag_class transition spatial_lag
##   <chr>           <int>   <int> <ord>     <chr>            <dbl>
## 1 Alabama          2010    2015 Q1        Q1 -> Q1        23243.
## 2 Arizona          2010    2015 Q3        Q3 -> Q3        26636 
## 3 Arkansas         2010    2015 Q1        Q1 -> Q1        22687.
## 4 California       2010    2015 Q3        Q4 -> Q4        26804.
## 5 Colorado         2010    2015 Q2        Q4 -> Q5        24939.
## 6 Connecticut      2010    2015 Q5        Q5 -> Q5        28602
lag_intervals(spatial)
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   class  lower  upper type       
##   <ord>  <dbl>  <dbl> <chr>      
## 1 Q1    21806. 24582. spatial_lag
## 2 Q2    24582. 25701. spatial_lag
## 3 Q3    25701. 26850. spatial_lag
## 4 Q4    26850. 28552. spatial_lag
## 5 Q5    28552. 33541  spatial_lag
transition_matrix(spatial, "count", lag_class = "Q1")
##    Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4 Q5
## Q1  4  2  0  0  0
## Q2  0  1  3  0  0
## Q3  0  0  1  0  0
## Q4  0  0  0  0  1
## Q5  0  0  0  0  0

The code below is intentionally optional because neither package is a hard dependency.

library(griddy)
library(estdaR)
library(spdyn)
library(spData)
library(spdep)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

data(us_states, package = "spData")
data(us_states_df, package = "spData")

states <- us_states |>
  left_join(us_states_df, by = c("NAME" = "state")) |>
  filter(!NAME %in% c("Alaska", "Hawaii", "Puerto Rico")) |>
  arrange(NAME)

wide <- states |>
  st_drop_geometry() |>
  select(NAME, median_income_10, median_income_15)

panel <- states |>
  select(NAME, median_income_10, median_income_15, geometry) |>
  pivot_longer(starts_with("median_income_"), names_to = "year", values_to = "income") |>
  mutate(year = if_else(year == "median_income_10", 2010L, 2015L))

nb <- poly2nb(states, queen = TRUE)
listw <- nb2listw(nb, style = "W")

grd <- panel |>
  classify_dynamics(NAME, year, income, k = 5) |>
  markov_dynamics(NAME, year, class)

estdar <- estdaR::mkv(as.matrix(wide[, c("median_income_10", "median_income_15")]), classes = 5, fixed = TRUE)
spdyn <- spdyn::markov(wide, stateVars = c("median_income_10", "median_income_15"), n.states = 5, pool = TRUE, std = FALSE)

transition_matrix(grd, "count")
estdar$Transitions
spdyn$t

Expected differences should be documented. Classification conventions and standardization defaults differ across packages, so exact equality requires matching those choices first. The package test suite uses static fixtures from estdaR::sp.mkv() and spdyn::spMarkov() so ordinary CI can validate the spatial Markov counts without installing either package.

Developed by Dmitry Shkolnik.

Site built with pkgdown 2.1.3.
